coredata.dc:title: Programmable ultrasound imaging guided theranostic nanodroplet destruction for precision therapy of breast cancer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Feihong
An, Jian
Guo, Wenyu
Dang, Jie
Huang, Shuo
Feng, Feng
Zhang, Jiabin
Wang, Di
Yin, Jingyi
Fang, Jing
Cheng, Heping
Zhang, Jue
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasound-stimulated contrast agents have gained significant attention in the field of tumor treatment as drug delivery systems. However, their limited drug-loading efficiency and the issue of bulky, imprecise release have resulted in inadequate drug concentrations at targeted tissues. Herein, we developed a highly efficient approach for doxorubicin (DOX) precise release at tumor site and real-time feedback via an integrated strategy of “programmable ultrasonic imaging guided accurate nanodroplet destruction for drug release” (PND). We synthesized DOX-loaded nanodroplets (DOX-NDs) with improved loading efficiency (15 %) and smaller size (mean particle size: 358 nm). These DOX-NDs exhibited lower ultrasound activation thresholds (2.46 MPa). By utilizing a single diagnostic transducer for both ultrasound stimulation and imaging guidance, we successfully vaporized the DOX-NDs and released the drug at the tumor site in 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice. Remarkably, the PND group achieved similar tumor remission effects with less than half the dose of DOX required in conventional treatment. Furthermore, the ultrasound-mediated vaporization of DOX-NDs induced tumor cell apoptosis with minimal damage to surrounding normal tissues. In summary, our PND strategy offers a precise and programmable approach for drug delivery and therapy, combining ultrasound imaging guidance. This approach shows great potential in enhancing tumor treatment efficacy while minimizing harm to healthy tissues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spontaneous Nucleation
DOX-Loaded Nanodroplets
Programmable Ultrasound Imaging
Acoustic Droplet Vaporization
Precision Therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The vaporization regulation of DOX-NDs by the design ultrasound sequence
The biocompatibility, cytotoxicity, and drug release of DOX-loaded NDs on cell experiments
Ultrasound imaging-guided drug release from DOX-NDs in vivo
The antitumor efficacy of DOX-NDs in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the efficient and accurate drug release at tumor site from DOX-NDs induced by a programmable diagnostic transducer.

Synthesis and characterization of DOX-NDs. (a) The schematic illustration of a DOX-ND demonstrates the core–shell structure. (b) The bright images of DOX-NDs for the DOX qualitative evaluation. (c) The TEM images of DOX-NDs for the morphology evaluation. (d) The size distributions of the NDs and DOX-NDs measurement by DLS. The inset shows the precipitated DOX-NDs (left) and NDs (right) pictures after dissolution in PBS. (e) The absorbance curves of NDs and DOX-NDs at the wavelength of 280–720 nm. (f) The drug encapsulation efficiency and drug loading efficiency of DOX-NDs. (g) B-mode imaging of the tube containing DOX-NDs with 2 × 109/mL at 200 mL/min under variable acoustic pressure. The image shows a high signal after the activation position (yellow mark) stimulated by a supra-threshold ultrasound. (h,i) The vaporization probability of DOX-NDs and NDs as a function of acoustic pressure with the respective fit sigmoid to determine ADV threshold. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The vaporization regulation of DOX-NDs by the design ultrasound sequence. (a) A schematic diagram of the agar phantom embedded uniformly distributed DOX-NDs imaging by a diagnostic probe. (b) The B-mode and PI-mode images of diluted DOX-NDs at 25 °C and 37 °C. (c) The graph of quantified gray-scale intensities in panel (b). (d, e) The PI-mode images of diluted DOX-NDs stimulated by variable ultrasound pressures and corresponding quantified gray-scale intensities. (f, g) The PI-mode images of DOX-NDs before and after ultrasound stimulation at 4.98 MPa under variable concentrations of DOX-NDs and corresponding quantified gray-scale intensities.

The illustration for the biocompatibility, cytotoxicity, and drug release of DOX-loaded NDs on cell experiments. (a) The cell viability of 4 T1 cells after co-incubation with DOX-ND at different concentrations of DOX. (b) The cell viability of 4 T1 cells treated by PBS, PBS + US, DOX + US, ND + US, DOX-ND + US at different concentrations of DOX. For the ultrasound stimulation groups, the tumor was exposed to ultrasound (1 MHz, 1 W cm−2, 50 % duty, 1 min) with Dundex, UT1041. (c) The fluorescence microscopy images of 4 T1 cells after co-incubation with DOX-NDs for 1 h, followed by ultrasound irradiation for 1 min ([DOX] = 10 μg/mL, blue fluorescence: DAPI, green fluorescence: DiO, red fluorescence: DOX). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Ultrasound imaging-guided drug release from DOX-NDs in vivo. (a) A schematic illustration of 4 T1 tumor xenograft establishment, treatment procedure, and outcome of therapy by using DOX-NDs@2 with ultrasound imaging-guided stimulation. (b) The two time-adjacent B-mode images (S1 and S2) of DOX-NDs burst in the tumor of different layers induced by ultrasound stimulation using the designed ultrasound sequence. The red signals are generated by the subtraction of the two adjacent images (S1-S2) under ultrasound stimulation, indicating the burst or vaporization (low probability) of the DOX-NDs induced by ultrasound pulses. (c) The quantified burst efficiency is defined as the ratio of the burst area and the whole tumor area (yellow circle) at different layers in b. (d) The B-mode images of DOX-NDs in the tumor at pre-ultrasound stimulation, the subtraction of the two time-adjacent images (SN+1-SN) during ultrasound stimulation, and the maximum intensity projection (MIP) images of the burst signal during 5 s stimulation in the largest layer of the tumor at three different stages during the treatment. (e) The corresponding quantified burst efficiency of DOX-NDs in d. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The antitumor efficacy of DOX-NDs for the accurate release of DOX under the guidance of ultrasound real-time imaging. (a) Tumor growth curves and (b) body weight curves of the mice with different treatments. The data were presented as the mean ± SD (n = 6), *P < 0.05, ***P < 0.001. (c) Histological analysis of the excised tumors was evaluated via H&E staining (upper), TUNEL assay (lower). Scale bar = 100 μm.

head.ce:title: Programmable ultrasound imaging guided theranostic nanodroplet destruction for precision therapy of breast cancer

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasound-stimulated contrast agents have gained significant attention in the field of tumor treatment as drug delivery systems. However, their limited drug-loading efficiency and the issue of bulky, imprecise release have resulted in inadequate drug concentrations at targeted tissues. Herein, we developed a highly efficient approach for doxorubicin (DOX) precise release at tumor site and real-time feedback via an integrated strategy of “programmable ultrasonic imaging guided accurate nanodroplet destruction for drug release” (PND). We synthesized DOX-loaded nanodroplets (DOX-NDs) with improved loading efficiency (15 %) and smaller size (mean particle size: 358 nm). These DOX-NDs exhibited lower ultrasound activation thresholds (2.46 MPa). By utilizing a single diagnostic transducer for both ultrasound stimulation and imaging guidance, we successfully vaporized the DOX-NDs and released the drug at the tumor site in 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice. Remarkably, the PND group achieved similar tumor remission effects with less than half the dose of DOX required in conventional treatment. Furthermore, the ultrasound-mediated vaporization of DOX-NDs induced tumor cell apoptosis with minimal damage to surrounding normal tissues. In summary, our PND strategy offers a precise and programmable approach for drug delivery and therapy, combining ultrasound imaging guidance. This approach shows great potential in enhancing tumor treatment efficacy while minimizing harm to healthy tissues.

ce:section.ce:para: Drug delivery systems utilizing microbubble (MB) carriers have been extensively studied for several decades [1]. MBs not only serve as imaging contrast agents [2] but also function as carriers for drug delivery [3,4]. These carriers can be destructed under ultrasound stimulation to release the loaded drug within the diseased tissues. Furthermore, the millions of cavitation events resulting from MB destruction can mechanically disrupt the dense extracellular matrix, facilitating deeper tumor penetration of the drug [5–7]. Compared to traditional nanoparticle drug delivery systems, ultrasound-responsive drug carriers rely on ultrasound cavitation to achieve active release and accumulation of drugs at the target site, controllably improving the utilization efficiency of drugs[8]. In the past decade, considerable attention has been given to versatile MBs, which show promise as theranostic carriers for real-time ultrasound contrast imaging [9,10], and ultrasound-triggered drug release[5,6,11]. However, typical tumors have fenestrate openings ranging from 400 nm to 600 nm [12], making it difficult for MBs with a size of several microns to penetrate the leaky tumor vasculature and target cancer cells [13]. Consequently, nanobubbles (NBs), which are submicron bubbles with diameters typically ranging from 200 nm to 600 nm, have emerged as more attractive candidates for drug delivery. NBs exhibit significantly enhanced tumor accumulation and retention[14–18] compared to MBs. However, the use of MBs or NBs as popular drug delivery vehicles has certain limitations. Conjugating drugs onto their shell surfaces often results in lower drug encapsulation efficiency (generally less than 10 %) [19]. Additionally, the encapsulation of drugs into MBs or NBs leads to relatively shorter circulation times in the bloodstream. These disadvantages pose challenges in achieving efficient drug concentration at targeted tissues, thereby impeding their applications in drug delivery [20,21].

In recent years, phase-change nanodroplets (NDs) containing a liquid core of perfluorocarbon (PFC) have emerged as a promising alternative. These NDs offer a combination of high tissue permeability and extended circulation time, compared with microbubbles and nanobubbles [22]. Moreover, due to their smaller size and enhanced stability, NDs exhibit superior performance in penetrating the internal regions of tumors [23,24]. When converged in tumor sites, the NDs could be further vaporized to microbubbles under targeted external ultrasound trigger[22,25,26], termed as acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV). Subsequently, these newly formed microbubbles oscillate or explode, facilitating the release of therapeutic drugs encapsulated within them to target tumor cells[24]. Due to their unique properties, phase-change NDs have garnered interest for various applications in cancer imaging and therapy, including extravascular ultrasound imaging [27–29], drug delivery[30,31], and vascular embolization[32–37]. As the essential link of ADV for drug delivery, ultrasound stimulation acts as a form of longitudinal mechanical wave that can be easily transmitted inside the human body, and has been widely used in tumor imaging and therapy[21,38]. However, it should be noted that while diagnostic ultrasound in clinical settings induces microbubble collapse and expansion, achieving vaporization of NDs into MBs and triggering local drug release requires higher levels of ultrasound energy, which comes at the expense of losing imaging guidance. Moreover, the high intensity of ultrasound energy in the focal area can inadvertently damage normal tissues along the ultrasound path, leading to severe side effects such as transient pain and nerve injury [39]. Therefore, there is a pressing need to develop effective and safe modalities for tumor-specific imaging and therapy.

To overcome these challenges, we present an integrated strategy, “programmable ultrasonic imaging-mediated accurate release with high drug loading nanodroplets” (PND), utilizing a single diagnostic ultrasound transducer (Scheme 1 ). Initially, we incorporated perfluoropentane (PFP) into the NDs' core, significantly lowering the vaporization threshold while maintaining stability. Next, we employed an alternative ouzo method, leveraging spontaneous nucleation [40], to synthesize NDs that encapsulate doxorubicin (DOX), a potent anthracycline topoisomerase II inhibitor [41], used in effective cancer treatments. For precise tumor-specific imaging and drug delivery guided by ultrasound imaging, we devised a programmable ultrasound sequence on the Verasonics platform. We evaluated the biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of DOX-encapsulating nanodroplets (DOX-NDs) with high drug encapsulation efficiency in normal PIEC cells and breast tumor 4 T1 cells. Furthermore, we investigated the therapeutic efficacy of DOX-NDs in tumor-bearing mice, guided solely by ultrasound imaging using a diagnostic transducer.

In search of an ideal drug delivery system for the efficient and accurate release of drugs from NDs mediated by diagnostic ultrasound, we fabricated DOX-loaded nanodroplets (DOX-NDs) using the modified ouzo method and well designed custom sequences for the tumor-specific imaging and drug delivery on the platform of Verasonics. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the designed DOX-NDs include DSPC, DSPE-PEG2000, pluronic, DOX, and PFP, which are elaborately integrated to achieve theranostic and synergistic functionality. Especially, DSPC with highly biocompatible and biodegradable has comprised the shell of nanodroplets; DSPE-PEG-2000 is used to modify the outer layer of shells, avoiding phagocytosis of the nanodroplets by immune cells; the surfactant pluronic F68 is admixed to adjust the surface tension to help the stability of NDs. The liquid PFP with a lower boiling point of 29 °C is encapsulated in the core of NDs, which ensures the vaporization of NDs stimulated by the diagnostic ultrasound; while DOX as the anticancer drug is loaded into the nanocomposites for enhanced chemotherapy.

The synthetic process of DOX-NDs was based on the modified ouzo methods (Fig. S1) due to the spontaneous formation of anise oil droplets when water is added to the alcoholic drink. Two types of NDs, including DOX-NDs and unloaded NDs were fabricated in this study. The optical image in Fig. 1b and TEM morphology in Fig. 1c demonstrate the core–shell spherical structure of DOX-ND. Meanwhile, based on the dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement in Fig. 1d, the average diameter of DOX-ND and ND are around 358 nm and 382 nm with narrow size distributions, respectively, indicating the encapsulation of DOX into the NDs does little effect on the diameter of NDs. These results indicate that the NDs carrying the DOX were small enough to pass through the endothelial gaps (380–780 nm) of defective blood vessels surrounding the tumors.

Furthermore, according to the particle size of NDs and the amount of perfluoropentane liquid added, the concentration of NDs can be estimated to be about 1.8 × 10 11/ml, which presents two orders of magnitude higher than the working concentration of MBs’ drug delivery system. It is worth noting that the higher concentration and larger specific surface provide higher drug loading efficiency. Besides, the absorption spectra of NDs and DOX-NDs at 280–700 nm have been detected in Fig. 1e, showing that the DOX-NDs have a strong absorption peak at about 485 nm, while it is almost invisible for NDs. Thus, the drug loading efficiency and drug encapsulation efficiency of DOX-NDs were estimated through the DOX standard curve at 485 nm. As shown in Fig. 1f, it can be clearly observed that at the initial drug concentration of 1.5 mg/ml, the drug encapsulation efficiency can reach 25 %. At the same time, the drug loading efficiency can be as high as 15 %, far exceeding the drug loading rate of microbubbles (generally about 3–5 %), which offers a prerequisite guarantee for DOX-NDs to play an excellent anti-tumor effect in vivo. We have also evaluated the ADV thresholds of DOX-NDs and NDs. Fig. 1g demonstrates the vaporization process of DOX-NDs in the tube, as focus activated and imaged by the linear transduce L12-3 V at B-mode. When the applied pressure is above a given threshold, the DOX-NDs will be vaporized to microbubbles and further flow inside the tube after the activation position, generating highly echogenic signals on Activated ROI at B-mode images. The ratio of echo-power data between Background and Activated ROI as a function of acoustic pressure is well fitted to a sigmoidal model in Fig. 1h and i. As a result, the ADV thresholds obtained were 2.46 MPa and 2.48 MPa for DOX-NDs and NDs, respectively, which ensures the vaporization of DOX-NDs to release the drugs induced by the diagnostic ultrasound.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The vaporization regulation of DOX-NDs by the design ultrasound sequence
ce:section.ce:para: The acoustic characteristics of DOX-NDs have been investigated using an agar phantom embedded uniformly distributed DOX-NDs at variable concentrations, as stimulated and imaged by the linear transducer L12-3 V (Fig. 2 a). Firstly, the B-mode imaging and contrast-enhanced ultrasonography (PI-mode) imaging at 0.72 MPa were used to evaluate the stability of DOX-NDs at 25 °C and 37 °C. The images on both B-mode and PI-mode exhibit dark signals in Fig. 2b, indicating that as expected, the DOX-NDs present a satisfactory stability at 25 ℃. Furthermore, we raised the temperature to 37 ℃ to indirectly verify the stability of DOX-NDs at human body temperature. As shown in Fig. 2b, although exceeding the boiling point of perfluoropentane (29 ℃), the DOX-NDs are not vaporized, probably due to the existence of Laplace pressure existed on the surface of nanodroplets. Besides, the grayscale intensity of the phantom under B-mode and PI-mode is at a much lower level in Fig. 2c, which also proves the satisfied stability of DOX-NDs.

We further adjusted the acoustic pressure to optimize the setting that triggers vaporization of DOX-NDs by regulating the voltage of the ultrasound sequence and acquiring ultrasound images of the phantom in PI mode. As shown in Fig. 2d, the appearance of bright signal can be easily observed in those images with the increase of the voltage of the ultrasound sequence. That means that the designed pulse sequence can successfully induce DOX-NDs to vaporize into microbubbles that produce strong echoes as real-time feedback. Meanwhile, depending on the size distribution of DOX-NDs, the echo intensity exhibits enhancement with the increase of ultrasonic voltage. More specifically, those relatively larger DOX-NDs are more easily vaporized at the lower acoustic pressure. (Fig. 2e). Besides, the paired PI mode ultrasound images before and after ultrasound stimulation were compared at 4.98 MPa under variable concentrations of DOX-NDs. Both Fig. 2f and 2 g show that there was no significant difference in intensity of images even with the expansion of concentration before ultrasound stimulation; while after ultrasound stimulation, the increase of bright signal in the ultrasonic image can be observed. More importantly, the enhancement of echo signal intensity can be also observed in the ultrasonic images with the increase of DOX-NDs concentration, which is indeed caused by the formation of microbubbles by the vaporization of DOX-NDs. As expected, the above results show that the ultrasonic pulse can effectively induce the vaporization of DOX-NDs and its extent can be evaluated through ultrasonic images as feedback, which provides a valuable potential for accurate release of drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The biocompatibility, cytotoxicity, and drug release of DOX-loaded NDs on cell experiments
ce:section.ce:para: The therapeutic effect of DOX-NDs was explored on PIEC cells (normal cell line) and 4 T1 cells (tumor cell line). Firstly, we evaluated the biocompatibility of DOX-NDs at different concentrations incubated with cells for 1 h. As shown in Fig. S3, the incubation time of 1 h has little effect on the growth of PIEC cells, and the viabilities of PIEC cells have no significant difference with the increase of DOX-NDs concentration.

Actually, the DOX-NDs need to be injected intravenously to achieve the drug delivery at the targeted site and our results indirectly indicate that DOX-NDs will not result in obvious toxic side effects on endothelial cells within 1 h at blood vasculature. The window period of 1 h can offer sufficient time for ultrasonic stimulation to accurately drugs release from DOX-NDs at the tumor site. Besides, the experiment of incubating DOX-NDs with 4 T1 cells further confirms the safety of DOX-NDs without ultrasound stimulation, as shown in Fig. 3 a, and corresponding results demonstrate that the DOX-NDs will not affect the survival activity of 4 T1 cells and the corresponding viabilities present no significant changes with the increase of DOX-NDs concentration. Then, we investigated the effect of ultrasound stimulation on the viability of 4 T1 cells at different DOX concentrations. Fig. 3b shows that the viability of the ND + US group is not significantly different from that of the control group at low concentration (5 μg/ml) group, and the ultrasound alone does not damage the survival activity of tumor cells. While interestingly, once the concentration of NDs is increased (>10 μg/ml), the inhibitory effects of ND + US on tumor cells will appear and even become comparable with DOX + US groups, indicating that simple droplet gasification and destruction can produce obvious biological effects to inhibit tumor cell growth, which is actually consistent with the previous results.[42,43] It is understandable, as well as greatly discussed in previous studies on drug delivery research regarding microbubbles, mechanical action caused by the oscillating bubbles that stretch and tear the nearby cell membrane will lead to possible cell edema, cell dissolution, and eventually death,[44,45] which further in turn illustrates the necessity of sufficiently accurate vaporization of droplets in human body. For the DOX group, it has an obvious dose relationship between inhibition efficiency and DOX dose, which has been determined by the well-recognized mechanism of DOX on tumor cells. For the DOX-ND + US group, it is observed that DOX-ND + US have the strongest killing effect on 4 T1 cells under the same equivalent DOX concentration, and show significant differences compared with other control groups. Although ND + US will not inhibit the survival rate of 4 T1 cells at a low concentration, the cavitation effect produced by the DOX-ND + US can change the permeability of cells, which increases the contact chances between cell membranes and drugs, thus enhancing the absorption of DOX for cells.

It is worth noting that the inhibitory effect of DOX-ND + US is more obvious compared with other groups mainly due to the cavitation effect at high concentration. To our surprise, the viability has no significant difference between DOX-ND + US group at 10 μg/ml and DOX group at 20 μg/ml, indicating that the DOX-ND + US can indeed achieve a comparable therapeutic effect to that only the half dose of the DOX takes. This provides ample evidence that the proposed PND strategy can allow “remarkably reducing the dose while maintaining therapeutic effect”.

We further observed the release of DOX from DOX-NDs with and without ultrasound stimulation through a confocal microscope. The cytoplasm and nucleus of 4 T1 cells were stained with fluorescent DiO and DAPI respectively to detect and locate cells. Although the DOX-NDs and cells were incubated for 1 h, no red fluorescence can be observed without ultrasound stimulations in Fig. 3c. This indicates that the drug on the DOX-NDs cannot easily enter the cells and affect the survival of cells, which is consistent with the safety result of DOX-NDs on 4 T1 cells. Meanwhile, the red fluorescence could be observed in the DOX-ND + US group, indicating that ultrasound treatment can achieve vaporization and then release drugs into the tumor cell. These above research results demonstrate that DOX-NDs with satisfied stability can provide a sufficient time window for the accurate and controllable release of drugs at the tumor site through appropriate ultrasound stimulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasound imaging-guided drug release from DOX-NDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The tumor suppression experiments of DOX-NDs were conducted using the 4 T1 cell tumor-bearing mouse model, then the customized ultrasound sequence was applied to achieve precise DOX delivery from DOX-NDs, which is in an image-guided, noninvasive, and spatiotemporally controllable manner.

The treatment was started on the 10th day from tumor inoculation, once every three days for three consecutive times in Fig. 4 a. The Imaging Sequence and Stimulation-Imaging Sequence (see the material and method and Fig. S2 for the details) were combinedly employed to realize the targeted release of DOX at the tumor site. As shown in Fig. S2, the pre-ultrasound images by using the imaging sequence can first obtain the tumor shape without triggering vaporization of DOX-ND, and the ROI of activation can be set according to the tumor shape. Then the activation-imaging sequence was used to stimulate and image the tumor and activation ROI. The DOX-NDs in the tumor site can be vaporized to microbubbles and subsequently burst under the focus-wave ultrasound stimulation, so as to exhibit the flash of light signals in the ultrasound images (Movie S1). The subtraction of the time-adjacent B-mode images (S1 and S2) also demonstrates the condition of DOX-NDs burst and then could infer the effect of drug release from DOX-NDs in Fig. 4b. As shown in Fig. 4c. the burst efficiency of DOX-NDs under ultrasound stimulation could be quantified by comparing the burst signals area and the whole tumor area at different layers. Furthermore, Fig. 4d demonstrated that even if the size and shape of the tumor have remarkably changed at three different stages of the treatment, PND strategy could enable only the tumor area is filled with bright signals in the MIP images, indicating that the DOX-NDs indeed release DOX into the tumor site with high efficiency, as expected. And the corresponding quantified burst efficiency of DOX-NDs in Fig. 4e indicated that the drug from the DOX-NDs had been released to 42 % of the tumor area in the largest layer under the ultrasound stimulation at 16th day.

Besides, Fig. S4 presented the focused ultrasound field distributions in X, Y, and Z direction when the ultrasound pressure was set to 3.89 MPa. The size of the focal region was calibrated to be 1 mm (X-direction), 0.6 mm (Z-direction), and 3 mm (Y-direction). This means that one can achieve precise stimulation at millimeter-level precision by adjusting the focus position and effective range of the ultrasonic pulse in advance according to the corresponding tumor images at low voltage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The antitumor efficacy of DOX-NDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: During the experiment, the mouse weight and tumor size were recorded every two days to evaluate the therapeutic effect of DOX-ND@2 + US. Fig. 5 a shows that the tumor size of the control group increased sharply with time, while the tumor growth in the treatment groups were much slower than that in the control group. After 14 days of DOX-ND@2 + US treatment, the tumor volume reduces to 45 % of that in the control group. It should be noted that the DOX-ND@2 + US treatment can achieve a similar therapeutic effect to the conventional DOX@5 treatment group, but with only less than half DOX dose. Fig. 5b shows that the weight of mice in DOX@5 group is lower than DOX-ND@2 + US group, indirectly proving that the lower dose treatment can reduce the toxic side effects of DOX while achieving a considerable therapeutic effect.

More importantly, the tumor inhibition effect of DOX-ND@2 + US is significantly improved than that of DOX-ND@2 group. DOX-NDs can be greatly enriched at the tumor site through passive EPR targeting, and the ultrasound administration with customized sequence can further induce the DOX-ND inside the tumor tissue and flowing through the tumor blood vessels to release the drug accurately under the ultrasound imaging guidance. Besides, the mechanical effect of DOX-NDs due to phase change and cavitation can also remarkably improve the diffusion of drugs around the tumor tissue, thus improving the utilization efficiency of drugs by tumor cells and avoiding the accumulation and leakage of drugs in normal tissues, which means that the visualization mediated by ultrasound imaging has a promising capability to significantly reduce the systemic toxic side effects of DOX-NDs. Furthermore, the tumor tissues were stained with HE and TUNEL to evaluate the histological characteristics of breast cancer under different treatment conditions after 24 days of tumor growth. As demonstrated in Fig. 5c, the DOX-ND@2 + US group exhibited significant nuclear deficiency and expression manifested as brown granules in cell nuclei compared with the control group, indicating efficient tumor cell apoptosis, which completely obeys the pharmacology of DOX. Furthermore, Fig. S5 reveals no apparent anatomical damage to the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys of mice before and after DOX-ND@2 + US treatment on the 9th and 17th day, underscoring the therapeutic potency and safety of our nanodroplet delivery system. Notably, the use of a dose that was only 0.4 times the traditional treatment group further validates the potential of our approach in minimizing toxicities and side effects while maintaining therapeutic efficacy. These results further illustrate that the visualization mediated by ultrasound imaging can accurately release DOX into tumor tissue. Actually, the cavitation effect caused by ultrasound-mediated microbubble fragmentation can act on the neovascularization of surrounding tumor tissues, resulting in the inhibition of neovascularization.[46] The related processes involve a variety of mechanisms, such as heat and cavitation. It is reasonable to speculate that the micro shear force generated by nanodroplet vaporization and explosion after vaporization probably led to the destruction of the microvascular system, so as to allow more efficient drug delivery, inhibit the proliferation of tumor vascular endothelial cells and tumor cells, and achieve satisfied therapeutic effects.[21,37].

In conclusion, we proposed, developed an integrated PND strategy, enabling ultrasound imaging-mediated accurate drug release from DOX loaded NDs for tumor therapy. More importantly, the lower ADV threshold by using perfluoropentane as the core enables that DOX-NDs to be vaporized and accurately release the DOX at tumor tissue under the image-guided manner only using a diagnostic probe by the customized sequence. The DOX-ND@2 + US treatment with only less than half DOX dose can achieve a similar therapeutic effect to that for the conventional drug group. Meanwhile, the ultrasound-mediated DOX-NDs will not cause damage to other tissues and organs, but will only cause cell apoptosis at the tumor site. In general, our study made a bold attempt from the aspects of contrast agent preparation and intervention design, to effectively overcome the common pain points in the drug delivery field, that is, insufficient drug concentration at targeted tissue with low drug loading efficiency and inaccurate release. However, in this work, we present an alternative drug delivery pathway that only inhibits the growth of tumor tissue, rather than eliminate tumor tissue using the low dose of DOX. When combined with more effective drug, gene or protein, the integrated strategy could inspire more promising therapeutic potential in vascular-related disease therapy.
All materials, methods, and additional data can be found in the Electronic Supplementary Material.

Feihong Dong: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jian An: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Validation. Wenyu Guo: Investigation, Validation. Jie Dang: Investigation, Methodology. Shuo Huang: Investigation, Methodology. Feng Feng: Methodology. Jiabin Zhang: Methodology. Di Wang: Methodology, Software. Jingyi Yin: Methodology, Software. Jing Fang: Writing – original draft. Heping Cheng: Supervision, Writing – original draft. Jue Zhang: Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31327901, 31821091, 81571666). Feihong Dong was supported in part by the Postdoctoral Fellowship of Peking-Tsinghua Center for Life Sciences.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data Includes the experimental section, the illustration of synthetic process for DOX-NDs and customized ultrasound pulse sequences, the biocompatibility of DOX-NDs, acoustic pressure and distribution of ultrasound pulses, H&E staining of main organs studies and the B-mode images of DOX-NDs burst in tumor site.
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2024.106854.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary figure 1
The synthetic process for DOX-NDs using spontaneous nucleation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Customized ultrasound pulse sequences. (a) The illustration of customized Imaging Sequence used. (b) The illustration of customized Stimulation-Imaging Sequence used. Note that plane-wave imaging guidance to locate and delimit the tumor's boundaries. The drug delivery via spatiotemporally programmable scanning of focus-wave pulses to trigger DOX-ND vaporization and destruction. The height of the histogram represents the intensity of the ultrasound-focused pulse and plane wave pulse.
The cell viability of PIEC cells (normal cell line) after co-incubation with DOX-NDs at different concentrations of DOX.
(a) Three-dimensional intensity distributions of the focus ultrasound pulse. (b)The intensity profiles of the full-width- half maximum in the X, Y, and Z directions.
Histological analysis of the excised tumors for H&E staining on the 9th day (before treatment) and 17th day (after treatment).
